nodes.label: Abolhassan Banisadr
nodes.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)

data.role: 1st President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1980–1981)
data.bio: Abolhassan Banisadr was the first president of the Islamic Republic, elected in January 1980 with 76% of the vote. An economist and political thinker who had been close to [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in exile, he quickly came into conflict with the clerical establishment and the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). He was impeached by parliament in June 1981 and fled to France, where he lived in exile until his death in 2021.

nodes.label: Ahmad Jannati
nodes.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts

data.role: Secretary of the Guardian Council (1992–present); former Chairman of Assembly of Experts
data.bio: Ahmad Jannati has been a member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) since its founding in 1980, and its secretary since 1992 — the longest-tenured figure in any constitutional body of the Islamic Republic. A founding member of the hardline [Haghani Seminary](/orgs/haghani-seminary) in Qom alongside [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), he is among the most extreme voices in Iranian politics, repeatedly calling for the execution of political opponents and systematically disqualifying reformist candidates. He served concurrently as Chairman of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). His continuation in office into his late nineties has been attributed to political calculation over capacity.

nodes.label: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani
nodes.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)

data.role: 4th President of Iran (1989–1997); Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (1989–2017)
data.bio: Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani was one of the most powerful figures in post-revolutionary Iran. He served as Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) from 1980 to 1989 and as president from 1989 to 1997. A cleric and confidant of [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), he is associated with the pragmatist wing of Iranian politics and championed post-war economic reconstruction. He chaired the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) until his death in January 2017.

nodes.label: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1992–2000); Head of Supreme Leader's Inspection Office
data.bio: Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri served as Speaker of Parliament for two full terms, 1992 to 2000. He was the establishment-backed frontrunner in the 1997 presidential election before being unexpectedly defeated by reformist [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) in a landslide. He subsequently served as head of the Inspection [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader)'s office. He represents the traditional conservative wing of Iranian politics.

nodes.label: Ali Khamenei
nodes.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)

data.role: 2nd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1989–2026)
data.bio: Ali Khamenei served as Iran's third president from 1981 to 1989, then succeeded [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) as Supreme Leader following Khomeini's death. As Supreme Leader for nearly four decades, he exercised ultimate authority over all branches of government, the military, and the judiciary. He oversaw Iran's nuclear programme, the crackdown on the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009, and the response to the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022. He died on 28 February 2026.

nodes.label: Ali Larijani
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator
data.bio: Ali Larijani is the longest-serving speaker in the history of the Islamic Republic, holding the position from 2008 to 2020. He previously served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2005–2007) and secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council). He was disqualified from the 2021 presidential election by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). His brother [Sadegh Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) served as head of the judiciary.

nodes.label: Ali Motahari
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)
data.bio: Ali Motahari is the son of the martyred Islamic scholar [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) and the brother-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani). He represented Tehran for three consecutive terms (8th–10th Majlis, 2008–2020), serving as Second Deputy Speaker in the 10th Majlis (2016–2019). A principlist by formal affiliation, he was widely regarded as a maverick who publicly challenged [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad), criticised [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) political interference, and called for the release of [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) leaders. He was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from the 2020 elections. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran.

nodes.label: Ali Nikzad
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)
data.bio: Ali Nikzad is a conservative civil engineer and politician from Ardabil Province who has served as Second Deputy Speaker of the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf across two consecutive sessions (2020–present). He previously served as Minister of Roads and Urban Development under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2011–2013) and has been a member of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council).

nodes.label: Alireza Arafi
nodes.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Alireza Arafi is a senior cleric and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member appointed by Khamenei in 2019. He has served as president of the Islamic Seminaries of Iran (Hawza). Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he was appointed as the Guardian Council's representative on the three-member interim leadership council, alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and [Chief Justice Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei), pursuant to Article 111 of the constitution. He has been mentioned as a potential future Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate
data.bio: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi is a hardline politician from Mashhad who served as First Deputy Speaker of the 11th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf (2020–2024). He was one of only seven candidates approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from 585 applicants in the 2021 presidential election, finishing fourth. A physician by training, he represents the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned principlist networks that swept the 11th Majlis.

nodes.label: Behzad Nabavi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR

data.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR
data.bio: Behzad Nabavi is a senior figure of the Islamic left turned democratic reformist. An electrical engineer, he fought against the Shah and was imprisoned before the revolution. He co-founded the Islamic Revolution Committees (komiteh) and a forerunner to the Ministry of Intelligence. Under [PM Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) he served as minister for executive affairs and minister of heavy industry. He was also Iran's chief negotiator in the 1981 [Algiers Accords](/events/algiers-accords-1981) that ended the hostage crisis. In the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) he alternated as First Deputy Speaker with [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 elections and sentenced to six years.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Raisi
nodes.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)

data.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)
data.bio: Ebrahim Raisi was a hardline cleric who served as head of the judiciary from 2019 before being elected president in 2021 in an election with record-low turnout. He is alleged to have been a member of the 'Death Commission' that ordered the mass execution of political prisoners in 1988. He was subject to US Treasury sanctions since 2019. His presidency was dominated by the Mahsa Amini protests of 2022. He died on 19 May 2024 when his helicopter crashed in foggy conditions near the Azerbaijan border.

nodes.label: Esmail Qaani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)
data.bio: Esmail Qaani succeeded [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) as [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander on the day of Soleimani's assassination, appointed immediately by Khamenei. A veteran of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and longtime Quds Force deputy, his tenure was marked by criticism that he lacked Soleimani's strategic flair and public profile. He oversaw Quds Force operations during the Gaza conflict from 2023 and the broader escalation with Israel. Iranian sources reported he was killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.bio: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel is a philosopher and politician who served as Speaker of Parliament from 2004 to 2008, the first non-cleric to hold the role since the 1979 revolution. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran and has led the Iranian [Academy of Persian Language and Literature](/orgs/iranian-academy-of-persian-language) since 1995. His daughter Zahra married [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei), son of the Supreme Leader, making Haddad-Adel one of the most personally connected figures to the supreme leadership. He is a senior member of the principlist [Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran).

nodes.label: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei is a hardline jurist appointed Chief Justice by Khamenei in July 2021. He previously served as Minister of Intelligence under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2005–2009), as prosecutor-general, and as first deputy chief justice. He is regarded as a reliable enforcer of the regime's security priorities. Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he became one of three members of the constitutional interim leadership council alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member Arafi.

nodes.label: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)
data.bio: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee is a conservative politician from Hamadan Province serving as First Deputy Speaker of the 12th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under [Speaker Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf). He previously served as Minister of Education under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2009–2013). A long-serving principlist MP, he represents the established traditional conservative wing of the Majlis.

nodes.label: Hassan Rouhani
nodes.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)

data.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)
data.bio: Hassan Rouhani was elected in 2013 on a centrist platform promising to ease sanctions. He oversaw negotiations that led to the 2015 [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal, the signature achievement of his presidency. After the United States withdrew from the JCPOA in 2018, Iran's economy deteriorated sharply. Prior to the presidency he served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2003–2005) and as secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) for sixteen years.

nodes.label: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian
nodes.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024)

data.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024)
data.bio: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian served as Iran's Minister of Foreign Affairs under President Ebrahim Raisi from 2021 until his death in the helicopter crash of May 2024. A career diplomat close to the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), he had previously served as deputy foreign minister for Arab and African affairs. He was killed alongside [President Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) when their helicopter crashed in foggy conditions in East Azerbaijan Province.

nodes.label: Hossein Salami
nodes.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)

data.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)
data.bio: Hossein Salami served as Commander-in-Chief of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from April 2019, appointed by Khamenei following the removal of [Mohammad Ali Jafari](/people/mohammad-ali-jafari). He joined the IRGC in 1980 at the outbreak of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and rose through its Aerospace Force before appointment to the top post. He oversaw the IRGC during the Mahsa Amini crackdown of 2022, the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile and drone programmes, and the direct Iranian strikes against Israel in April 2024. He was reportedly killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Mohammad Javad Zarif
nodes.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)

data.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)
data.bio: Mohammad Javad Zarif served as Iran's Foreign Minister throughout [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s presidency, leading the Iranian negotiating team that concluded the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal in 2015. A US-educated career diplomat, he was Iran's ambassador to the United Nations before becoming foreign minister. He endorsed [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in the 2024 presidential election and briefly served as vice president for strategic affairs before resigning. He was subject to US sanctions from 2019.

nodes.label: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad
nodes.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)

data.role: 6th President of Iran (2005–2013)
data.bio: Mahmoud Ahmadinejad served as mayor of Tehran from 2003 before his presidential election victory in 2005. His two-term presidency was defined by confrontational nuclear rhetoric, Holocaust denial, economic mismanagement, and the disputed 2009 election that triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He significantly expanded the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s economic and political power. After leaving office he fell out with Khamenei and was disqualified from subsequent presidential races.

nodes.label: Masoud Pezeshkian
nodes.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)

data.role: 9th President of Iran (2024–present)
data.bio: Masoud Pezeshkian, a cardiac surgeon and long-serving MP for Tabriz, won the snap presidential election of July 2024 following Raisi's death. He ran on a moderate platform and was endorsed by reformist figures including [Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and [Zarif](/people/javad-zarif). Ethnically Azerbaijani, he is the first non-Persian-speaking president. He previously served as Minister of Health under Khatami (2001–2005).

nodes.label: Mehdi Karroubi
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (1989–1992, 2000–2004); Green Movement leader
data.bio: Mehdi Karroubi served as Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) twice. A presidential candidate in 2009, he refused to accept the disputed official results and became a co-leader of the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) alongside [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi). He has been held under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011 without trial, one of the most prominent political detainees in the Islamic Republic.

nodes.label: Mir-Hossein Mousavi
nodes.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader

data.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran (1981–1989); Green Movement co-leader
data.bio: Mir-Hossein Mousavi served as Iran's last prime minister, managing the economy through the entire [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). An architect and painter by training, he was a founding figure of the Islamic Republic before gradually moving into opposition. His disputed loss in the 2009 presidential election triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He has been under house arrest in Tehran since February 2011. In 2023 he called for a referendum to fundamentally change the constitution, a significant shift from his earlier reformist position. He is a distant relative of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) through a shared ancestral line from Khameneh.

nodes.label: Mohammad Ali Jafari
nodes.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (2007–2019)

data.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (2007–2019)
data.bio: Mohammad Ali Jafari served as Commander-in-Chief of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from 2007 to 2019. He reorganised the IRGC's command structure and expanded its internal security role, particularly the [Basij](/orgs/basij) militia. He oversaw IRGC operations during the Syrian civil war and the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile programme. He was replaced by [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) in April 2019.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Ali Rajai
nodes.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)

data.role: 2nd President of the Islamic Republic of Iran (August 1981)
data.bio: Mohammad-Ali Rajai was Iran's second president, serving for less than one month after his election on 24 July 1981. A committed revolutionary and former teacher, he had previously served as prime minister. He and his prime minister [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar) were assassinated in a bombing at the prime minister's office on 30 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK).

nodes.label: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2020–present); Former Mayor of Tehran (2005–2017)
data.bio: Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf is a senior [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) figure who commanded the IRGC Aerospace Force and later the national police before serving as Mayor of Tehran for twelve years. He has run for president three times without success. Elected Speaker of Parliament in 2020 after the principlist sweep of that year's elections. He was one of 24 IRGC commanders who signed a 1999 letter threatening military action against student protesters, and has admitted to personally participating in street-level violence against them.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Javad Bahonar
nodes.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1981)

data.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1981)
data.bio: Mohammad-Javad Bahonar was a cleric and co-founder of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). He served briefly as prime minister under [President Mohammad-Ali Rajai](/people/mohammad-ali-rajai) in 1981. He and Rajai were assassinated in a bombing at the prime minister's office on 30 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK). He had previously served as Minister of Education.

nodes.label: Mohammad Khatami
nodes.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)

data.role: 5th President of Iran (1997–2005)
data.bio: Mohammad Khatami was elected president in a landslide in 1997, winning around 70% of the vote on a platform of civil society, rule of law, and a Dialogue Among Civilizations. His two terms represented the peak of the reformist movement, marked by a free press and attempts to improve relations with the West. His reform agenda was consistently blocked by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and judiciary.

nodes.label: Mohammad Mokhber
nodes.role: First Vice President (2021–2024); Acting President (May–July 2024)

data.role: First Vice President (2021–2024); Acting President (May–July 2024)
data.bio: Mohammad Mokhber served as First Vice President under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from 2021 until Raisi's death in the helicopter crash of May 2024. He then served as acting president for fifty days until [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) was elected. Previously he headed the Setad (Execution of [Imam Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s Order), one of the largest and most opaque conglomerates in Iran, controlled directly by the Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Mohammad-Reza Khatami
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (2000–2004); Secretary-General, Islamic Iran Participation Front

data.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (2000–2004); Secretary-General, Islamic Iran Participation Front
data.bio: Mohammad-Reza Khatami is a nephrologist and reformist politician, the younger brother of [President Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). He entered politics after his brother's 1997 victory, becoming the top-polling candidate in Tehran in the March 2000 election. He served as Deputy Speaker of the reformist 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) alternating with [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi). He founded and served as Secretary-General of the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front), Iran's largest reformist party. He is married to Zahra Eshraghi, a granddaughter of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini). He was permanently disqualified from running for office by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

nodes.label: Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi
nodes.role: Ultra-conservative cleric and ideologue

data.role: Ultra-conservative cleric and ideologue
data.bio: Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi was an ultra-conservative Shia cleric and one of the most polarising ideologues of the Islamic Republic. He co-founded the [Haghani Seminary](/orgs/haghani-seminary) in Qom alongside [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), which trained a generation of hardline clerics and security officials. He was a member of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) and a key ideological backer of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad). He was known for justifying violence against political opponents and opposing democratic reform. He died on 1 January 2021.

nodes.label: Mohsen Armin
nodes.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis

data.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis
data.bio: Mohsen Armin is a reformist politician, journalist, and key figure in the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as a member of parliament in the reformist 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He has been active in political journalism and the reform movement, advocating for political openness and democratic governance within the Islamic Republic framework.

nodes.label: Mojtaba Khamenei
nodes.role: Son of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei; unofficial power broker

data.role: Son of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei; unofficial power broker
data.bio: Mojtaba Khamenei is the second son of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and widely reported to be one of the most powerful behind-the-scenes figures in the Islamic Republic. He is alleged to have played a central role in the crackdown on the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009 and to coordinate between the Supreme Leader's office and the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij). He married a daughter of [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), cementing ties between the supreme leadership and the conservative parliamentary establishment. He has been subject to US and EU sanctions.

nodes.label: Morteza Motahhari
nodes.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman

data.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman
data.bio: Morteza Motahhari was one of the most influential clerical scholars of the Islamic Revolution. A devoted student of [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in Qom, he co-founded the Hussainiyyah Ershad and the [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) (JRM). He served as chairman of Khomeini's Council of the Islamic Revolution in early 1979. He was assassinated on 1 May 1979 by the [Forqan Group](/orgs/forqan-group). His intellectual legacy underpins much of the Islamic Republic's ideological framework. He is the father-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and the father of MP [Ali Motahari](/people/ali-motahari).

nodes.label: Mostafa Tajzadeh
nodes.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister

data.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister
data.bio: Mostafa Tajzadeh is a prominent reformist politician and member of the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as deputy interior minister under [President Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests and sentenced to six years in prison. He attempted to run for president in 2021 but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). He was re-arrested in 2022 during the Mahsa Amini protests.

nodes.label: Qasem Soleimani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)
data.bio: Qasem Soleimani rose from a working-class background in Kerman to become the architect of Iran's network of regional proxies — [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah), Shia militias in Iraq, the Houthis, and forces in Syria. As [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander from 1998 until his assassination, he was widely described as the most powerful military operative in the Middle East. He directed Iranian intervention in Syria from 2012 and was the primary coordinator of Iran's Axis of Resistance. He was killed on 3 January 2020 in a US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport, authorised by President Trump.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Khomeini
nodes.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran

data.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran
data.bio: Ruhollah Khomeini was the Shia cleric and marja who led the 1979 [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), overthrowing the Pahlavi monarchy and establishing the Islamic Republic. He served as Supreme Leader from 1979 until his death in June 1989. He developed the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist), which forms the constitutional basis of clerical rule in Iran.

nodes.label: Sadegh Larijani
nodes.role: Head of the Judiciary (2009–2019); Chairman of the Expediency Discernment Council

data.role: Head of the Judiciary (2009–2019); Chairman of the Expediency Discernment Council
data.bio: Sadegh Larijani served as head of Iran's judiciary from 2009 to 2019, appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). His tenure coincided with the crackdown on the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) and the prosecution of political dissidents. He subsequently became chairman of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council). He is the brother of [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), the former Speaker of Parliament, making the Larijani family one of the most powerful political dynasties in the Islamic Republic.

nodes.label: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran

data.name_en: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran
data.description: The Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran (Abadgaran) was a conservative-principlist political coalition formed in 2003. Described as 'Iran's neocons', it was composed mainly of [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij) veterans who had been marginalised under [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and [Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). It swept the 2003 municipal and 2004 parliamentary elections — helped enormously by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s mass disqualification of reformist candidates — and backed [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s successful 2005 presidential run. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker from 2004–2008, was a key figure.

The Assembly of Experts (Majles-e Khobregan-e Rahbari) is an 88-member body of senior clerics elected by popular vote for eight-year terms. Its constitutional mandate is to select, supervise, and if necessary dismiss the Supreme Leader. All candidates must be vetted by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). In practice, it has never exercised its supervisory or dismissal powers. It is chaired by a speaker elected from among its members.

The Association of Combatant Clerics (Majma'-e Rowhaniyoun-e Mobarez, MRM) broke away from the conservative [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) in 1988. It is the reformist counterpart in clerical politics, associated with the Islamic left and later the reform movement. Key figures include [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) and [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) (the president). It supports political liberalisation within an Islamic framework.

The Basij Resistance Force (Niroo-ye Moqavemat-e Basij) is a paramilitary volunteer militia subordinate to the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). Founded by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1979, it played a prominent role in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) through human-wave assaults. It functions as an internal security and social control force, operating through mosques, universities, and government offices. It was centrally involved in the violent suppression of the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009 and the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022.

The Combatant Clergy Association (Jame'e-ye Rowhaniyat-e Mobarez, JRM) is a conservative clerical political organisation founded in 1977 by a group of clerics including [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It is one of the oldest and most powerful political bodies in the Islamic Republic, consistently aligning with the conservative and principlist factions. Key affiliated figures include [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and [Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri](/people/ali-akbar-nategh-nouri). It split in 1988 when its left wing broke away to form the [Association of Combatant Clerics](/orgs/association-of-combatant-clerics) (MRM).

The Expediency Discernment Council of the System (Majma'-e Tashkhis-e Maslahat-e Nezam) was established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1988 to resolve legislative disputes between the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It also acts as an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. Membership is appointed by the Supreme Leader and typically includes former presidents, senior clerics, and military figures, making it a powerful gathering point for Iran's political elite.

The Forqan Group (Goruh-e Forqan) was a small militant Islamist organisation that carried out a series of assassinations in the chaotic months following the 1979 revolution. It assassinated [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) on 1 May 1979 and attempted to kill [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). The group opposed clerical rule, arguing for a return to the Quran without clerical intermediaries. Its members were arrested and executed by 1980.

The Guardian Council (Shura-ye Negahban) is a 12-member constitutional body with three mandates: vetting legislation for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution; supervising elections; and approving or disqualifying all candidates for parliament, presidency, and [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). Six members are Islamic jurists appointed directly by the Supreme Leader; six are civil lawyers nominated by the Chief Justice (also appointed by the Supreme Leader) and confirmed by parliament. Members serve six-year terms in staggered cohorts.

The Haghani Seminary (Madrasa-ye Haghani) is a hardline Islamic seminary in Qom co-founded by [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati) and [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi). It has trained a disproportionate number of the Islamic Republic's intelligence, security, and judicial officials. Graduates are heavily represented in the judiciary, the Ministry of Intelligence, and the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It is considered one of the most ideologically extreme training grounds in the Shia seminary system.

Hezbollah (Party of God) is a Lebanese Shia Islamist political party and militant group founded with the support of Iran's [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) during the Lebanese Civil War. It is the most powerful of Iran's regional proxies and receives extensive financial, military, and logistical support from the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). It operates as both a political party in Lebanese parliament and a military force with an arsenal that surpasses the Lebanese Armed Forces.

Hussainiyyeh Ershad is a religious and cultural institution in Tehran co-founded by [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It became famous in the 1960s and 1970s as a centre for modernist Islamic lectures, notably by Ali Shariati, whose talks attracted large audiences and contributed to the intellectual groundwork of the Islamic Revolution. It was closed by the Shah's government in 1973 but resumed activity after the revolution.

nodes.label: Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.name_en: Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.description: The Academy of Persian Language and Literature (Farhangestan-e Zaban va Adab-e Farsi) is Iran's official language-regulatory body, tasked with preserving and developing the Persian language. It coins Persian equivalents for foreign terms and oversees linguistic standards. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel) has served as its president since 1995.

The Quds Force (Jerusalem Force) is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) branch responsible for extraterritorial operations, unconventional warfare, and support to non-state allies. It reports directly to the Supreme Leader. The Quds Force supports [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) in Lebanon, Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad in Gaza, the Houthis in Yemen, and Shia militias in Iraq and Syria. It has been described by the US military as analogous to a combination of the CIA and JSOC.

The Islamic Consultative Assembly (Majles-e Shura-ye Eslami), commonly known as the Majlis or Majles, is the unicameral legislature of the Islamic Republic of Iran. It currently has 290 seats, elected for four-year terms. All candidates must be vetted and approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). The Majlis can legislate, ratify treaties, and impeach the president, but all legislation must pass Guardian Council review for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution.

The Islamic Iran Participation Front (Jebhe-ye Mosharekat-e Iran-e Eslami), abbreviated IIPF, was the largest and most prominent reformist political party in post-revolutionary Iran. Founded in 1998 by [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami), it was the main vehicle for the reformist 2nd Khordad movement that swept the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) in 2000. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) barred all IIPF candidates from running in the 2004 parliamentary election, effectively eliminating the party from parliament. It was formally suspended by the government in 2010 following the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests. The party advocated political pluralism, civil society, rule of law, and engagement with the West.

The Islamic Republican Party (IRP) was the dominant political party of the early Islamic Republic, founded in 1979 by a group of clerics including Mohammad Beheshti, [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), and [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). It was instrumental in consolidating clerical power and marginalising secular and leftist revolutionary factions, including President [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr). Its headquarters were bombed in 1981, killing Beheshti and over 70 others. It was dissolved in 1987 on [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s recommendation after internal factional disputes.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), known domestically as Sepah or Pasdaran, is a multi-service elite military force established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in May 1979. It consists of five branches: Ground Forces, Aerospace Force, Navy, [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), and [Basij](/orgs/basij). Its commander-in-chief reports directly to the Supreme Leader. Beyond its military role, the IRGC controls a vast economic empire including Khatam al-Anbiya Construction, multiple banks, and extensive holdings in energy, telecommunications, and construction. Estimated at 125,000 personnel.

Khatam al-Anbiya Construction Headquarters is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s engineering and construction arm and one of Iran's largest conglomerates. Originally formed to rebuild infrastructure after the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), it grew into a sprawling enterprise with interests in oil and gas, transportation, water management, and construction. It has been subject to US and EU sanctions for its role in Iran's nuclear and missile programmes and its ties to the IRGC.

The Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK), also known as the People's Mojahedin Organization of Iran (PMOI), is an Iranian opposition group that combines Islamism with Marxist ideology. It participated in the 1979 revolution but was violently suppressed by the new Islamic Republic. It was responsible for the 1981 bombing that killed [President Mohammad-Ali Rajai](/people/mohammad-ali-rajai) and Prime Minister [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar). It relocated to Iraq under Saddam Hussein's patronage and fought alongside Iraq in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). It is now based in Albania and advocates regime change.

nodes.label: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization

data.name_en: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization
data.description: The Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization (OMIIR) is a reformist party reconstituted in 1991 from the left wing of the original 1979 organisation of the same name. It is closely allied with the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front) within the reform movement. Key figures include [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi), [Mohsen Armin](/people/mohsen-armin), and [Mostafa Tajzadeh](/people/mostafa-tajzadeh). The party emphasised the republican dimensions of the Islamic Republic over its theocratic elements. Several members were arrested and imprisoned after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests.

The National Trust Party (Hezb-e E'temad-e Melli) was founded in 2005 by [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) after he came third in the first round of the presidential election that year. It positioned itself as a reformist but pragmatic party advocating social freedoms, economic reform, and political pluralism. Karroubi ran for president again under the party's banner in 2009. After the disputed election result and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), the party's leadership was suppressed and Karroubi himself was placed under house arrest.

The Office of Prime Minister was the head of government position in the Islamic Republic from 1979 to 1989. Under the 1979 constitution, executive power was divided between the president and the prime minister. The role was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, which merged its powers into the presidency. [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) was the last holder of the office.

The Presidency of the Islamic Republic of Iran is the second-highest office in the political system, below the Supreme Leader. The president is elected by popular vote for four-year terms, with a two-term consecutive limit. The president chairs the cabinet and is responsible for implementing the constitution. The post of prime minister was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, merging its powers into the presidency.

The Office of the Supreme Leader (Daftar-e Maqam-e Mo'azzam-e Rahbari) is the institutional apparatus of the Supreme Leader of Iran. The Supreme Leader holds ultimate authority over all branches of government, the armed forces, the judiciary, and state media under the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih. The office encompasses numerous subsidiary bodies including the Supreme Leader's Inspection Office, his representatives in the provinces, and his representatives to the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and other military branches.

The Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF, or Hashd al-Sha'abi) is an Iraqi state-sponsored umbrella organisation of predominantly Shia paramilitary groups, many of which receive training, funding, and direction from Iran's [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). Its deputy commander Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis was killed alongside [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) in the January 2020 US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport.

The Supreme National Security Council (SNSC) is Iran's top security and foreign policy decision-making body, chaired by the president. Its decisions require ratification by the Supreme Leader. Members include the heads of the three branches of government, the chief of the Supreme Command Council of the Armed Forces, military commanders, and the foreign and interior ministers. The secretary of the SNSC is one of the most powerful positions in Iran.

data.name: Algiers Accords
data.description: The Algiers Accords were agreements between Iran and the United States, mediated by Algeria, that resolved the Iran hostage crisis and secured the release of 52 American embassy staff held in Tehran for 444 days. [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi) served as Iran's chief negotiator. The accords were signed on 19 January 1981, and the hostages were released on 20 January, minutes after Ronald Reagan's inauguration as US president.

data.name: 1989 Constitutional Revision
data.description: The 1989 constitutional revision amended the Constitution of the Islamic Republic shortly before [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s death. Key changes included abolishing the position of prime minister and merging its powers into the presidency, establishing the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) and the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) as constitutional bodies, removing the requirement that the Supreme Leader be a marja (senior religious authority), and expanding the Supreme Leader's powers. The revision was approved by referendum on 28 July 1989, the same day as the presidential election that brought [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) to office.

data.name: Green Movement Protests
data.description: Following the disputed June 2009 presidential election in which incumbent [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) was declared the winner over reformist candidates [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), millions of Iranians took to the streets in the largest protests since the 1979 revolution. The government response was violent; the [Basij](/orgs/basij) and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) killed dozens of protesters. The movement was suppressed by late 2009, and its leaders Mousavi and Karroubi were placed under house arrest in 2011.

data.name: Iran-Iraq War
data.description: The Iran-Iraq War (1980–1988) was an eight-year conflict initiated by Iraq's invasion of Iran in September 1980. It was the longest conventional war of the twentieth century, killing an estimated 500,000 to 1 million people on both sides. The war shaped a generation of Iranian military and political leaders, particularly those who went on to lead the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) served as prime minister throughout the conflict. The war ended with UN-brokered ceasefire Resolution 598, which [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) accepted, comparing it to 'drinking poison'.

data.name: Iranian Revolution
data.description: The Islamic Revolution of 1979 overthrew the Pahlavi monarchy and established the Islamic Republic of Iran. Following months of mass protests, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi fled Iran on 16 January 1979. [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) returned from exile on 1 February 1979. The revolutionary government took power on 11 February 1979, the date now commemorated as the Day of Victory of the Islamic Revolution. A constitutional referendum in March confirmed the Islamic Republic, and a new theocratic constitution was approved in December 1979.

data.name: JCPOA Nuclear Deal
data.description: The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) was a landmark nuclear agreement reached on 14 July 2015 between Iran and the P5+1 (the United States, United Kingdom, France, Russia, China, and Germany). Iran agreed to limit its nuclear programme in exchange for the lifting of international sanctions. [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) led the Iranian negotiating team under President [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani). The United States withdrew from the agreement in May 2018 under President Trump, reimposing sanctions and triggering a severe economic downturn in Iran.

data.name: Assassination of Ali Larijani
data.description: Former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker and senior establishment figure [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) was killed in an Israeli airstrike on Tehran on 17 March 2026. His son Morteza Larijani and office chief Alireza Bayat were also killed in the attack. The killing came weeks after the death of [Supreme Leader Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and amid an ongoing Israeli-Iranian conflict that had severely degraded Iran's military and nuclear infrastructure. Iran officially declared Larijani a martyr. The assassination removed one of the Islamic Republic's most experienced diplomatic and legislative figures at a critical moment of regime transition.

sources.title: Iran's wartime leader isn't a dealmaker

data.name: Mahsa Amini Protests (Woman, Life, Freedom)
data.description: Following the death of 22-year-old Mahsa (Zhina) Amini in morality police custody on 16 September 2022, nationwide protests erupted under the slogan Woman, Life, Freedom (Zan, Zendegi, Azadi). The protests were the most sustained unrest in the history of the Islamic Republic, continuing for months across all provinces. Security forces killed over 500 protesters according to human rights organisations. Over 15,000 people were arrested. The protests were characterised by women publicly removing and burning their headscarves.

data.name: 1988 Mass Executions of Political Prisoners
data.description: In the summer of 1988, following the end of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), the Islamic Republic carried out the mass execution of thousands of political prisoners across Iran. The killings were ordered by a fatwa from [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) and carried out by 'Death Commissions' (committees of three or four officials) in prisons throughout the country. Estimates of those killed range from 2,800 to 30,000. [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) is alleged to have served on the Tehran Death Commission. The executions targeted primarily members of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) and leftist groups.

data.name: 2000 Iranian Parliamentary Election (6th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2000 parliamentary election produced a landslide victory for the reformist coalition aligned with [President Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). Reformist parties, led by the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front), won approximately 195 of 290 seats. It was the high-water mark of the reform movement: the Islamic Iran Participation Front's [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami) topped the Tehran ballot with nearly 1.8 million votes. The 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly), led by [Speaker Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), was the most reformist parliament in the Islamic Republic's history and was in constant conflict with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

data.name: 2004 Iranian Parliamentary Election (7th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2004 parliamentary election was marked by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s mass disqualification of approximately 2,400 candidates, including most reformist incumbents and all candidates of the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front). Approximately 80 sitting MPs were barred from seeking re-election. The result was a sweeping conservative victory, with the [Abadgaran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran) coalition winning over 190 seats. Speaker [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel) was elected with 226 of 259 votes, becoming the first non-clerical Speaker since the revolution. Turnout collapsed to around 51%.

data.name: 2020 Iranian Parliamentary Election (11th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2020 parliamentary election produced another conservative sweep, with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) disqualifying over 9,000 candidates including many moderate incumbents. Turnout was a record low of approximately 42% nationally and around 25% in Tehran. The principlist coalition won a large majority, and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned figure [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf) was elected Speaker days later. The election represented the full consolidation of hardliner control over the legislature.

data.name: 1997 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 1997 Iranian presidential election resulted in a landmark victory for reformist candidate [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami), who won approximately 70% of the vote against establishment favourite [Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri](/people/ali-akbar-nategh-nouri). The election shocked the Iranian political establishment and is considered the beginning of the reformist era. It demonstrated the significant gap between popular preferences and the ruling establishment's expectations.

data.name: 2005 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 2005 presidential election resulted in the surprise victory of Tehran mayor [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) over [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) in a run-off. Ahmadinejad ran as a populist outsider against the establishment, backed by the [Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran) and networks within the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij). [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), who initially led in first-round results before official tallies shifted, alleged fraud. The election marked the beginning of a sharp turn toward hardline populism.

data.name: 2009 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The June 2009 presidential election was one of the most consequential and disputed in the Islamic Republic's history. Incumbent [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) was declared the winner with 63% of the vote over reformist challenger [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). The opposition alleged massive fraud, and the disputed results triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) — the largest protests Iran had seen since the 1979 revolution. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) certified the results after a partial recount.

data.name: 2021 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 2021 presidential election was marked by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s disqualification of all major moderate and reformist candidates, including [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and [Mostafa Tajzadeh](/people/mostafa-tajzadeh). Only seven of 592 applicants were approved to run. [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) won with 62% of the vote, but turnout was a record-low 48.8%, with widespread calls for boycott. The election was widely seen as engineered to ensure Raisi's victory and consolidate hardliner control of all branches of government.

nodes.label: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash

data.name: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash
data.description: President [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi), Foreign Minister [Hossein Amir-Abdollahian](/people/hossein-amir-abdollahian), and six others died when their Bell 212 helicopter crashed in a fog-covered mountainous area near Jolfa in East Azerbaijan Province on 19 May 2024. The crash triggered a constitutional provision requiring a snap presidential election within fifty days. First Vice President [Mohammad Mokhber](/people/mohammad-mokhber) served as acting president. [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) was elected as successor on 5 July 2024.

data.name: Assassination of Qasem Soleimani
data.description: [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani), commander of the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), was killed in a US drone strike on his convoy outside Baghdad International Airport on 3 January 2020, authorised by President Trump. Also killed was Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, deputy commander of Iraq's [Popular Mobilization Forces](/orgs/popular-mobilization-forces). Iran vowed retaliation and launched ballistic missile strikes on US bases in Iraq days later. [Esmail Qaani](/people/esmail-qaani) was appointed Quds Force commander the same day.
